title: Look-Alike Insulin Pens — A High-Alert Device Error (Tresiba vs Fiasp)
subtitle: IIM-style case study + the D.E.F.E.N.D. SOP for staff educators and patient training
authors: IIM-style case study + standard operating procedure — illustrative

patient.chiefComplaint: Accidental swap of basal (Tresiba) and rapid (Fiasp) insulin cartridges between identical reusable pens
pmh: Diabetes on multiple daily insulin (basal + bolus regimen)
Uses reusable cartridge pens that look identical

caseDescription: A patient using reusable insulin pens accidentally loaded a Tresiba (ultra-long-acting basal) cartridge into the Fiasp-labelled pen, and vice versa. Because the reusable pens look completely identical, the patient unknowingly injected the wrong insulin profiles — a classic high-alert medication error that can cause severe, life-threatening hypoglycaemia (if a large basal-sized dose of rapid insulin is taken) or dangerous hyperglycaemia/DKA (if basal coverage is lost). The error is best understood as a system failure: when devices look identical, human error is inevitable unless it is engineered out. The intervention is the D.E.F.E.N.D. SOP — staff fail-safes (color-coding, tactile marking, teach-back) plus patient behavioural rules (One-at-a-Time, Read the Glass not the Cap, Distinct Storage), with the option of transitioning to manufacturer color-coded pre-filled disposable pens to remove the risk entirely.
keyFindings: Identical-looking reusable pens → cartridges swapped between Tresiba (basal) and Fiasp (rapid)
Wrong insulin profiles injected → risk of severe hypoglycaemia (insulin shock) or hyperglycaemia/DKA
Both cartridges changed at the same time; patient relied on the pen cap, not the glass cartridge label
High-alert medication + look-alike device = an error to be engineered out, not merely warned against
finalDiagnosis: Look-alike, high-alert medication error — basal (degludec/Tresiba) vs rapid (aspart/Fiasp) insulin cartridge swap in identical reusable pens; mitigated by the D.E.F.E.N.D. SOP
learningObjectives: Recognise look-alike insulin devices as a high-alert error source and the hypo/DKA risks of a basal–bolus swap
Apply root-cause analysis (design, process, training flaws) and engineer fail-safes (color-coding, tactile marking, teach-back)
Train patients on the One-at-a-Time, Read-the-Glass, and Distinct-Storage behavioural rules

items.title: The case: identical pens, swapped insulins
items.content: A patient on a basal–bolus regimen uses two reusable insulin pens — one for Tresiba (insulin degludec, ultra-long-acting basal) and one for Fiasp (insulin aspart, rapid-acting bolus). Because the reusable pens are physically identical, the patient loaded the Tresiba cartridge into the Fiasp pen and vice versa while changing both at once, and then injected the wrong insulin profiles. This is a textbook ‘look-alike’ high-alert medication error: the devices gave the patient no reliable way to tell them apart at the moment of use.
keyPoints: Basal (degludec/Tresiba) and rapid (aspart/Fiasp) cartridges were swapped between identical pens.
Both cartridges were changed at the same time, enabling the mix-up.
A look-alike high-alert error — not simple carelessness.

items.title: Why swapping basal (Tresiba) and rapid (Fiasp) insulin is so dangerous
items.content: The two insulins have opposite jobs and kinetics. Degludec (Tresiba) is an ultra-long-acting basal given in a steady once-daily dose; aspart (Fiasp) is a rapid-acting bolus given in larger, meal-matched doses. If the patient takes a basal-sized dose using the rapid pen (or injects rapid insulin thinking it is basal), the result can be severe, life-threatening hypoglycaemia (insulin shock). Conversely, if basal coverage is lost — rapid insulin loaded where long-acting was intended — the patient is left with dangerous hyperglycaemia and risk of diabetic ketoacidosis. Insulin is a high-alert medication precisely because these swaps are both easy to make and potentially fatal.
keyPoints: Tresiba = ultra-long basal (steady dose); Fiasp = rapid bolus (larger, meal-matched).
Basal-sized dose of rapid insulin → severe hypoglycaemia/insulin shock.
Loss of basal coverage → hyperglycaemia and DKA risk.

items.title: Root cause analysis: design, process, and training flaws
items.content: Three failures align. Design flaw (look-alike devices): the reusable pens lack sufficient visual or tactile differentiation, so a cognitive mix-up during cartridge replacement is easy. Process flaw: the patient changed both cartridges at the same time, with both boxes open, multiplying the chance of a swap. Training gap: the patient was taught to rely on the pen’s outer cap or label rather than verifying the insulin name printed on the glass cartridge itself. Fixing only one layer is insufficient; the safe system addresses all three.
keyPoints: Design: identical pens with no visual/tactile difference.
Process: both cartridges changed at once, both boxes open.
Training: reliance on the cap instead of reading the glass cartridge.

items.title: How educators engineer out the error (color-code, tactile-mark, teach-back)
items.content: The educator’s job is to make the mistake physically difficult. Protocol 1 — Mandatory color-coding: ensure the patient uses two different-coloured pens (e.g. a blue pen for Tresiba, a silver/red pen for Fiasp). Protocol 2 — Tactile modification: if matching coloured pens are unavailable, physically alter the devices before the patient leaves — wrap bright medical tape or a coloured rubber band on the rapid (Fiasp) pen, with a clinic-wide mnemonic such as ‘Rapid has the Rubber band’ or ‘Fast is Red’. Protocol 3 — Teach-back verification: never just tell the patient; ask them to demonstrate — ‘Show me how you will change the cartridge when your Fiasp runs out.’
keyPoints: Color-code: different-coloured pens for basal vs rapid.
Tactile-mark the rapid pen (tape/rubber band) with a clinic mnemonic.
Teach-back: have the patient demonstrate the cartridge change.

items.title: How patients prevent the swap (One-at-a-Time, Read the Glass, Distinct Storage)
items.content: Three behavioural rules remove ‘autopilot’ errors. Rule 1 — One-at-a-Time: never have both pens disassembled or both cartridge boxes open at the same time; change one pen completely, put its box away, then address the second. Rule 2 — Read the Glass, not the Cap: pen caps get swapped easily, so the ultimate source of truth is the insulin name printed on the glass cartridge in the pen window — physically read ‘Fiasp’ or ‘Tresiba’ before attaching the needle. Rule 3 — Distinct Storage: keep backup cartridges in separate areas of the fridge (e.g. Tresiba left shelf, Fiasp right) and store the active pens in different pouches or locations.
keyPoints: One-at-a-Time: change one pen fully before opening the other.
Read the Glass cartridge label, not the swappable cap.
Store the two insulins in distinctly separate places.

items.title: Why disposable pre-filled pens may remove the risk, and how to evaluate
items.content: The most definitive fix is to design the cartridge-handling step out altogether: manufacturer pre-filled disposable pens are distinctively colour-coded and are not reloaded, so there is no cartridge to swap. Where reusable cartridge pens must continue, the educator should log this incident in the clinic’s ‘Near-Miss / Adverse Event’ registry and give every patient on multiple insulins a mandatory ~5-minute refresher on this SOP at the next follow-up. Safety is a continuous loop: engineer the device, train the behaviour, audit the near-misses, and re-educate.
keyPoints: Pre-filled, colour-coded disposable pens eliminate the cartridge-swap step.
Log every event in a near-miss/adverse-event registry.
Mandatory ~5-minute SOP refresher at each follow-up.

items.question: Why must a look-alike insulin device error be engineered out rather than just warned against?
items.answer: Because human attention is unreliable and insulin is a high-alert medication where a single swap can be fatal. When two devices look identical, even a careful, well-informed patient will eventually mix them up on ‘autopilot’, especially while doing two cartridge changes at once. Warnings depend on the person remembering at exactly the wrong moment; engineered fail-safes — different colours, tactile marks, reading the glass cartridge, or removing the cartridge step entirely with disposable pens — make the error physically hard or impossible to commit. Safe systems put the defence in the design and process, not solely in the user’s vigilance.
keyPoints: Insulin is high-alert; a single swap can be fatal.
Identical devices guarantee eventual ‘autopilot’ error.
Engineered fail-safes beat reliance on memory and vigilance.

items.question: How should an educator verify that a patient can safely change an insulin cartridge?
items.answer: Use teach-back, not tell-back. Rather than simply explaining the steps, ask the patient to demonstrate the whole sequence — for example, ‘Show me what you will do when your Fiasp runs out.’ Watch them physically read the name on the glass cartridge before attaching the needle, confirm they only have one pen and one box open at a time, and check they place the correct insulin back in its distinct storage spot. Teach-back surfaces misunderstandings the patient cannot self-report, lets the educator correct technique on the spot, and confirms genuine competence before the patient leaves.
keyPoints: Have the patient demonstrate, not just listen.
Watch for reading the glass cartridge and the One-at-a-Time rule.
Correct technique on the spot before they leave.

items.question: List the three patient behavioural rules and the three staff fail-safes that prevent this insulin swap.
items.answer: Patient rules: (1) One-at-a-Time — never have both pens or both cartridge boxes open at once; change one completely, put it away, then the other. (2) Read the Glass, not the Cap — verify the insulin name printed on the glass cartridge in the pen window before attaching the needle. (3) Distinct Storage — keep the two insulins in clearly separate places in the fridge and in different pouches. Staff fail-safes: (1) Color-code the devices (different-coloured pens for basal vs rapid). (2) Tactile-mark the rapid pen (bright tape or rubber band) with a clinic mnemonic. (3) Teach-back verification — have the patient demonstrate the cartridge change. Definitive option: transition to manufacturer colour-coded pre-filled disposable pens; log the event and give a 5-minute SOP refresher at follow-up.

items.stem: A patient mistakenly injects a basal-sized dose using the rapid-acting (Fiasp) pen. What is the most dangerous immediate consequence?
options: Mild transient hyperglycaemia
Severe, potentially life-threatening hypoglycaemia (insulin shock)
A local injection-site reaction only
No effect, as the doses are interchangeable
items.explanation: Rapid-acting insulin given in a large, basal-sized dose drives blood glucose down quickly and profoundly, risking severe hypoglycaemia (insulin shock). The basal and bolus insulins are not interchangeable, and the danger is immediate and potentially fatal.

items.stem: What is the best fail-safe an educator can apply at dispensing to prevent look-alike pen mix-ups?
options: Tell the patient to be more careful
Color-code or tactile-mark the two pens (different-coloured pens, or bright tape/a rubber band on the rapid pen)
Give both pens the same colour for consistency
Provide a longer instruction leaflet
items.explanation: Engineering visual/tactile difference into the devices — different-coloured pens, or tape/rubber band on the rapid pen with a clinic mnemonic — makes the swap physically hard to commit. Warnings and leaflets rely on vigilance and fail; identical colours make it worse.

items.stem: Which is the ‘ultimate source of truth’ a patient should check before attaching the needle?
options: The pen’s outer cap
The colour of the pen body alone
The insulin name printed on the glass cartridge in the pen window
The pharmacy bag label
items.explanation: Caps and outer labels are easily swapped, so the reliable check is the insulin name printed on the glass cartridge visible in the pen window — patients must physically read ‘Tresiba’ or ‘Fiasp’ there before injecting.

items.stem: The ‘One-at-a-Time’ rule means the patient should:
options: Inject both insulins from one pen
Never have both pens disassembled or both cartridge boxes open simultaneously; change one completely before the other
Only ever use one insulin per day
Change both cartridges together to save time
items.explanation: Doing both changes at once, with both boxes open, is exactly what enables a swap. The One-at-a-Time rule has the patient complete and put away one pen/box before opening the other, removing the opportunity for confusion.

items.stem: Which method best confirms a patient can safely manage their two insulin pens?
options: Reading the instructions aloud to the patient
Asking the patient to demonstrate the cartridge change (teach-back)
Handing over a leaflet
Asking ‘Do you understand?’ and accepting a yes
items.explanation: Teach-back — having the patient demonstrate the full sequence — reveals errors they cannot self-report and lets the educator correct technique before the patient leaves. Passive methods (reading aloud, leaflets, a yes/no check) do not confirm competence.

items.stem: What is the most definitive way to eliminate the cartridge-swap risk entirely?
options: Use larger warning stickers on reusable pens
Transition the patient to manufacturer colour-coded pre-filled disposable pens that are not reloaded
Ask the patient to memorise the cartridge colours
Store both pens in the same drawer
items.explanation: Pre-filled, colour-coded disposable pens remove the cartridge-handling step altogether, so there is no cartridge to swap — designing the error out is more reliable than stickers, memory aids, or shared storage.

items.stem: In the root-cause analysis, which factor is a PROCESS flaw (as opposed to a design or training flaw)?
options: The pens look physically identical
The patient changed both cartridges at the same time with both boxes open
The patient was taught to rely on the cap rather than the glass cartridge
Insulin is a high-alert medication
items.explanation: Changing both cartridges simultaneously is a process flaw. Identical pens are a design flaw, relying on the cap is a training gap, and insulin being high-alert is the inherent hazard — the analysis separates these layers so each can be fixed.

items.statement: Tresiba is an ultra-long-acting basal insulin and Fiasp is a rapid-acting bolus insulin.
items.explanation: True. Tresiba (insulin degludec) is an ultra-long-acting basal insulin; Fiasp (insulin aspart) is a rapid-acting bolus insulin — opposite roles and kinetics, which is why a swap is dangerous.

items.statement: Injecting a basal-sized dose of rapid-acting insulin can cause severe hypoglycaemia.
items.explanation: True. A large, basal-sized dose of rapid insulin drives glucose down sharply and can cause severe, life-threatening hypoglycaemia (insulin shock).

items.statement: The pen’s outer cap is the most reliable way to confirm which insulin is loaded.
items.explanation: False. Caps are easily swapped; the reliable check is the insulin name printed on the glass cartridge in the pen window.

items.statement: To save time, patients should change both insulin cartridges at the same time.
items.explanation: False. Changing both at once, with both boxes open, is exactly what enables a swap. The One-at-a-Time rule completes one pen before opening the other.

items.statement: Teach-back (having the patient demonstrate) is preferred over simply telling the patient what to do.
items.explanation: True. Teach-back confirms genuine competence and surfaces misunderstandings that passive instruction misses.

items.statement: Manufacturer pre-filled disposable pens are distinctively colour-coded and can eliminate the cartridge-swap risk.
items.explanation: True. Because pre-filled disposable pens are colour-coded and not reloaded, there is no cartridge to swap — the most definitive fix where feasible.

items.assertion: Color-coding or tactile-marking the two insulin pens is preferred over simply warning the patient to be careful.
items.reason: Look-alike, high-alert devices produce errors unless the mistake is engineered to be physically difficult to make.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Because identical high-alert devices will eventually be confused regardless of warnings, building visual/tactile difference into them (or removing the cartridge step) is the reliable defence — which is exactly why engineered fail-safes are preferred over verbal caution.

items.stem: Why must look-alike insulin device errors be engineered out rather than relying on warnings?
options: A) Because warnings are more cost-effective than design changes
B) Because human attention is unreliable and insulin is a high-alert medication where a single swap can be fatal
C) Because regulatory agencies mandate engineered solutions over warnings
D) Because patients prefer tactile modifications over visual warnings
items.explanation: Insulin is a high-alert medication where a single dose error can be fatal. Human factors research shows that even careful users may mix identical devices on 'autopilot,' making warnings dependent on unreliable memory. Engineered fail-safes (e.g., color differentiation, tactile markers) prevent errors physically rather than relying on vigilance.

items.stem: What is the best method for an educator to verify a patient's ability to safely change an insulin cartridge?
options: A) Use teach-back by asking the patient to demonstrate the entire process
B) Provide a verbal explanation and confirm understanding through a yes/no question
C) Observe the patient silently without verbal interaction
D) Ask the patient to verbally list the steps without physical demonstration
items.explanation: Teach-back with demonstration ensures the patient can perform the task correctly, identifies misunderstandings, and allows immediate correction of technique.
